I think that this the logic here is a kind of confusing to follow. I was thinking of ways to potentially clean it up and came up with this -

The values that we have are:
``data['permalink']`` - Link to the submission comments
``data['url_full']`` - Link that the submission points to
``data['url']`` - What is displayed on the screen

So in keeping with this convention, we should really add a new field for the post type. That way you don't have to hijack the ``data['url']`` for x-posts. I think Brobin was eluding to this also.

```python
if data['permalink'].split('/r/')[-1] == data['url_full'].split('/r/')[-1]:
    data['url_type'] = 'selfpost'
    data['url'] = 'selfpost'

elif reddit_link.match(data['url_full']):
    data['url_type'] = 'x-post'
    data['url'] = 'x-post via {}'.format(data['subreddit'])

else:
    data['url_type'] = 'external'
    data['url'] = data['url_full']
```

This also simplifies subreddit.py because you can go back to printing ``data['url']`` directly.

You are correct that you need to generate cassettes for all of the tests that have changed. Here's the gist how it works:

```
# Install test dependencies
pip install rtv[test]

# Run the test in read-only mode, using the existing cassette
py.test tests/test_content.py::test_content_subreddit_from_name[gilded]

# Run the test using your local reddit credentials, and generate a new cassette
py.test --record-mode=once --refresh-token=~/.local/share/rtv/refresh-token tests/test_content.py::test_content_subreddit_from_name[gilded]
```

Try doing that for all of the tests that you are touching (not the whole test suite) and commit the new cassettes to your branch.

I’ll check if we can do better but with this grammar-based parsing I don’t think we can: We have three parsing stages: tokenizing, lexing and parsing.
* We can’t handle it during tokenizing/lexing because discarding works on whole expressions, not single tokens. For example, `#_ [] 2` should be read as `2`, not `] 2`; i.e. we must "see" the list before discarding it.
* We can’t do it after parsing because discarding affects the way we parse data structures: `{:a #_ #_ 1 2 :b}` should be read as `{:a :b}`, not `{:a #_, #_ 1, 2 :b}`. `[#_ #_ 1 2 3]` can’t be parsed as `[#_(#_(1)) 2 3]`; it should be parsed as `[#_ 2 3]` then `[3]`.

Typically you'd not mix function definitions with module-level executed code. Rather, you'd define your functions and create a main() function, that you'd call at the end using a so-called `ifmain` block:
```
if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

That way we can unit test the functions in the module by importing it from somewhere else without running the script code accidentally.

I took the noise bit out of `GSObject.__init__` entirely, and also removed the ability to pass in a different `GSObject`.  (I'm not sure we really need the bit of `GSObject.__init__` that remains either (passing in an `SBProfile`), but that at least wasn't in the way so I just left it).

I decided not to go with a standard python `@property` for `GSObject.noise`, since that would have required a `noise.setter` method and `_noise` instance variable too (to enable users to attach their favorite `_BaseCorrelatedNoise` object to, e.g., `gaussian_galaxy.noise`).  I did find, however, that a [`lazy_property`](http://stackoverflow.com/a/6849299) would work.  It turns out that not only does this delay and cache the computation of an "attribute", due to minutiae of the python descriptor protocol, the cached value is even faster to access than a regular `@property` - I think just as fast as a regular attribute.

With this in place, I removed all the noise propagation bits from the transformation methods and compound constructors, and find that, for example, `sheared = real_galaxy.shear(g1=0.1)` goes down from ~40 microseconds to ~20 microseconds.  Fun :).

Finally, to squash a bug, I decided to have `RandomWalk` inherit from `GSObject` instead of `Sum`.  Despite being a sum of a bunch of Gaussians, `RandomWalk` doesn't really behave like a `Sum`.  In particular, there's no `self._obj_list` attribute, which some of the `Sum` methods depend on.  I think it's simpler to change the parent class than to keep overriding methods that work for `Sum` but not `RandomWalk`.

Est-ce qu'il est possible d'avoir un foyer fiscal sans déclarant principal ?

L'algorithme n'a pas changé:

Avant : 

```py
ppe = self.cast_from_entity_to_role(ppe_holder, role = VOUS)  # La PPE de chaque foyer fiscal projetée sur les individus, uniquement pour les personnes qui ont le role "VOUS", a.k.a les premiers déclarants de chaque fiscal. 
ppe = self.sum_by_entity(ppe)  # On somme le résultat sur tous les membres du ménage 
```

Maintenant:

```py
ppe_i = menage.members.foyer_fiscal('ppe', period)  # PPE du foyer fiscal auquel appartient chaque membre du ménage
ppe = menage.sum(ppe_i, role = FoyerFiscal.DECLARANT_PRINCIPAL)  # On somme seulement pour les déclarants principaux
```

En effet, s'il n'y a pas de déclarant principal dans un foyer fiscal, sa PPE ne sera pas prise en compte pour le ménage. Mais c'était déjà le comportement précédent.

This needs unification with `rhs`, but: the non-intuitive thing I discovered while implementing this is that in order to keep `Node` identities small (ie, containing the fewest Params possible), we want to prefer Entries that need fewer parameters. This biases toward avoiding propagating information from callers to callees unless they really need it to be executed (and avoids the case where absolutely anything used anywhere in the graph is potentially a parameter used at the root of the graph: that will continue to be bounded by `RootRule`).

In short: we would generally prefer a `@rule` to a Param, because depending on a rule does not affect our identity, while using a `Param` does (because it comes from the caller).

Hm... I see. I don't have any objections against keeping the PATH clean. However there is one important thing:

**There must be a (command line) way to put conda/activate on the PATH.**

In Visual Studio, there is always a `vcars*.bat` or `setenv.cmd` script on the PATH which allows you to quickly add the development environment to the shell you are currently working with. Although there is start menu command to open such a shell in a new terminal, it is *essential* to be able to do that from a script (e.g. Makefile).

For Anaconda, it could work like this:
1. Skip adding `Anaconda\Scripts` and `Anaconda` to the PATH when installing Anaconda.
2. Provide a `anaconda.bat` script in `Anaconda\somepath` which adds `Anaconda\Scripts` and `Anaconda` to the PATH.
3. Put `Anaconda\somepath` on the system PATH

This allows to easily provide a start menu entry (just call `cmd /k anaconda.bat`) but also to integrate Anaconda in custom batch scripts. In my company we have a lot of scripts like the following Jupyter notebook starter (simplified code):
```batch
if not exist "%~dp0venv" (
    conda create -p %~dp0venv --file=requirements.txt jupyter
)
call activate %~dp0venv
jupyter notebook
```
Simply by distributing this batch script together with a requirements file, we can provide the user a single entry point to installing and running Jupyter notebook. If you decide to drop Anaconda from the system path, we have to adapt this code as conda is not on the PATH. The easiest solution would be to add a `call anaconda.bat` at the beginning of the script.

The use of equality as the test here is slightly dangerous.  For instance, if I have an vhost entry like this:
```
Header add "Content-Security-Policy" "upgrade-insecure-requests"
```
then this test will return false, because it's comparing the strings `"upgrade-insecure-requests"` and `upgrade-insecure-requests`.  So you wind up with this in your conf file after `letsencrypt install --uir`:

```
Header add "Content-Security-Policy" "upgrade-insecure-requests"
Header always set Content-Security-Policy upgrade-insecure-requests
```
I don't know what a more correct alternative is, though.  Substring matching doesn't work either because there could be something like this in the conf file:
```
Header append Content-Security-Policy "script-src 'self' upgrade-insecure-requests.info"
``` 
We don't want to have to write parsers for every type of header we support, or at least not yet, so some zen philosophy might be required in working around this issue.

 - The `Namespace` knows how to perform this transformation

I, personally, prefer not to be too 'opinionated' when it comes to what 'requirements' a template imposes. My reasoning behind tools like this is too make sure that the execution of the script itself works. For example, the current implementation is very basic. All it does is to execute a python script. I would like to add stdout, stderr coming out of migration scripts to be displayed at execution time.

However, there are always common points in any migration. I do see a valid argument for defining some structure to the script like a `migrate` method so that the developers (even those new to python) can ignore the 'ceremony' of a python script and focus on the actual code. I do also see a valid argument on providing a Cassandra session by default because, believe or not, you may be interested on doing some Cassandra operations :D

Another thing is utility methods like the one that executes a query in your original code `q(...)`. So, wherever in my custom script, if I want to execute a query, all I need to do is call `q('SELECT * FROM keyspace.table')` without worrying about the internals.

But aside from that I don't see any other common intersections. I would leave it pretty much more open to the developer and less opinionated from a template perspective. 

For example, we have a case where we want to extract data from a table using the COPY command from cqlsh; then parse UDTs into regular columns; then execute a schema migration (new table with new columns) using the Cassandra session and last, import that data using either COPY or sstableloader tool or whatever gets the job done more efficiently.

As you can see I am using cqlsh, sstableloader, etc., which in many cases most people won't use.

I'll make those changes to the script template and commit them. Looking forward to more suggestions!
